data.title: LexinFintech: Macroeconomic Tailwinds Boosts Profits
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Last year, LexinFintech (LX) was trading at 2x forward P/E. Today, the valuation multiple is 4x forward earnings. This multiples expansion, combined with net income growth, has resulted in an extraordinary level of share price gain. The chances of this happening again in the next 12 months aren't slim. LX is experiencing a paradigm shift, with more focus on profitability instead of blindly pursuing growth. The pivot is likely in response to the policy signals from Beijing, seeking to calm the public's concerns and mistrust over the nation's new fintechs. But the real story for investors is that LX's earnings are rising, and management seems committed to returning excess cash to shareholders via dividends.

Revenue Growth

After a period of rapid expansion in the mid-2010s, growth in the digital consumer loan market is now driven by regulations. LX has been cleaning its balance sheet to maintain its compliance and license. New regulations in 2017 and 2021 put a cap on the interest that LX and its funding partners can charge borrowers to 24%. The new laws also set a limit on the size of online loans to RMB 200,000. Equally important, LX now faces restrictions on direct lending, forcing it to change its model to loan guarantees while collaborating with "funding partners". Sales performance has been mediocre in the past five years, with tighter regulations, combined with the pandemic disruptions, impacting LX's growth plans in the nascent online lending market.

In Q1 2025, LX sales stood at 3.1 billion, down 4.3% YoY, and their loan book principal balance (both direct lending and off-balance sheet loan guarantees) was $107 billion, down 12.3% YoY.

Management attributed the revenue decline to a "change in product mix", which refers to their inability to make direct loans, shifting their focus to loan guarantees instead of direct lending. Naturally, income from loan guarantees is less than a full-fledged lending service. I expect these dynamics to continue going into 2025.

However, while revenue might remain pressured, things are looking bright on the profitability front.

Net Income

In Q1 2025, LX's net income rose 113% on the back of credit quality improvements. Provisions for financing and guarantees declined 12.5%, from RMB 1.1 billion to RMB 989 million, in Q1 2025, pushing gross margins up 200 basis points, from 37% to 39%, the highest on record for the company. The +90-Day Delinquency rate fell to 3.3%, breaking a three-year upward trend. First-payment delinquency rate continued a downward trend that commenced last year.

In my view, LX's recent performance is a result of regulatory and macroeconomic tailwinds as opposed to idiosyncratic factors impacting LX alone. While LX's net income margin increase is a welcomed development, it wasn't unique to LX. All of the six major digital consumer loan companies have experienced gross margin appreciation in Q1 2025, suggesting an industry-wide tide that lifted all boats.

Valuation

At a 4x forward PE ratio, LX undoubtedly trades at a discount compared to the broader market. Whether it is undervalued is another story. When stacked against peers within China's Fintech arena, LX's valuation seems in line with the sector's average, mirroring the market's skepticism over its business model, the reliability of its algorithms, and the organizational and structural risks mentioned at the beginning of this piece.

What is interesting is that the price multiple nearly doubled, boosted by the market's optimism over China's central bank accommodative policies last September, and LX's management's decision to raise dividend payout ratios last November. Interestingly, LX's share price reaction to the surprise 113% increase in revenue was more subdued compared to the stock price surge when management announced the dividend boost. This is understandable, given the weight of the Principal-Agency problem facing Chinese companies that rely on a handful of individuals' goodwill, integrity, and reputation to protect their interests.

In my view, the primary driver of LX's shares is management's dividend policy, with EPS growth being a secondary factor.

Finally, when compared to other Fintechs in the US, LX's value doesn't seem off the charts in terms of PE multiples.

Capital Allocation

In 2017, the Chinese regulators passed the "Regulation on the Supervision and Management of Financing Guarantee Companies" - a law that limited LX's ability to leverage its balance sheet by setting a maximum cap on the loan portfolio it can guarantee to 10 times its book value. As of March 2025, LX had a book value of RMB 11.2 billion. For the sake of simplification, we'll ignore LX's direct lending business (or what's left of it) and loan reinsurance from third parties. Using the RMB 11.2 billion figure, LX can guarantee a loan book of RMB 112 billion. Today, it holds RMB 107 billion. With the company's capital fully deployed, growth will be a function of retained earnings and return on equity.

Management's decision to release Guarantee liabilities in Q1 2025 - the first time in many years - is a sign of favorable loss re-measurement. But, while there is a lot to be optimistic about, LX earnings and profitability metrics have been historically volatile. Management's payout policy of distributing 30% of net income instead of a fixed dividend aligns with this volatility and is designed to protect the capital buffer required by authorities. Based on average 11.3% ROE and a 70% retention rate, I believe LX's sustainable growth rate is roughly 7.9%.

Final Thoughts

LX's net income grew by more than 100% in Q1 2025, coinciding with management's de-risking initiatives that saw provisions for loan losses decline. This exceptional performance isn't unique to LX, with all six major loan facilitation fintechs experiencing YoY net income growth. This suggests that macroeconomic tailwinds played a role in LX's performance. Although there are signs that some of the profitability gains are sustainable, the allure of recent financial results dims when stacked against the company's historically volatile earnings and profitability ratios.

Equally important, with the recent increase in the dividend payout rate, and assuming an average 11.3 ROE, the company's sustainable net income growth rate is closer to 7.9%.

These dynamics lead us to conclude that the primary driver of valuation multiples expansion is the payout ratio. Our thesis is justified by the positive impact of dividends on the Principal-Agent problem facing LX, given its complex organizational structure, reliance on VIE, HFCAA Act (delisting risk) and heavy reliance on management's integrity and goodwill to protect shareholders' interests.

data.title: NMAI: Solid Multi-Asset CEF But Challenging Macro Environment
data.description:
Overview

Nuveen Multi-Asset Income Fund (NYSE:NMAI) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns through a blended portfolio of equities and debt investments. I've grown to like these blended funds because the income aspect, if executed correctly, can be an effective way to offset the volatility experienced in traditional market indexes. The continuous income received from the debt securities within a blended fund like NMAI, has the ability to provide investors with a steady stream of income during all market cycles.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that NMAI's price has only increased by less than 1%. However, the total return jumps to 15.2% when including all of the distributions that were paid out to shareholders over the same time frame. The whole point of maintaining exposure to a multi-asset fund like NMAI is for the high level of income that can be collected. For instance, NMAI offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 12.1%. In addition, NMAI issues its distributions out on a monthly basis, which can be ideal for retired investors looking to use dividend income to fund lifestyle expenses.

NMAI is still relatively new and has an inception dating back to November 2021. Therefore, we are working with limited operating data and the fund hasn't really been able to demonstrate what it's performance would look like through more 'normal' market conditions. However, the caveat is that NMAI was the result of a merger of three different Nuveen funds, which all had a different focus.

  • Nuveen Diversified Dividend and Income Fund
  • Nuveen Tax-Advantaged Total Return Strategy Fund
  • Nuveen Tax-Advantaged Dividend Growth Fund

The fund had unfortunate timing around its launch between the pandemic, changing interest rates, record-breaking inflationary data, and shifting consumer behavior. The challenges have caused the price to fall to a discount to NAV valuation. However, I believe that this may present an attractive opportunity for long-term investors.

Fund Strategy

Looking at the most recent fact sheet, we can see that NMAI has total managed assets of $625M that is allocated across approximately 1,337 different holdings. This large number of holdings are spread across a diverse range of industries and asset classes, which creates a unique blend for investors that want to get a bit of exposure to several focus areas. The diversity goes even further when we consider the fact that NMAI has a sizable allocation to investments outside of the US. The top five regions are:

  1. United States: 69%
  2. United Kingdom: 3.4%
  3. France: 2.9%
  4. Japan: 2.4%
  5. Canada: 2.0%

Regarding the breakdown of assets, we can see below that Common Stocks make up the largest percentage of assets at 43.6%. This is followed by exposure to Asset-Backed Securities and Senior Loans, accounting for 12.9% and 12.4% of assets, respectively. The combination of these three focus areas makes up approximately 69% of assets, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on NMAI's daily price change and total return potential.

The diverse range of assets means that the top holdings of NMAI are quite interesting. The fund is trying to strike a balance between growth and income generation, so it doesn't lean too heavily on any specific sector. iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (IEMG) is the largest holding, which is an ETF that invests in equities of global emerging regions around the world. This is followed by exposure to Prologis (PLD), which is a REIT, as well as Microsoft (MSFT) and Apple (AAPL).

The unfortunate part of the strategy is that NMAI is likely to underperform traditional indexes over the long term. Just as a quick comparison, we can see how NMAI underperforms traditional index-tracking ETFs like SPDR S&P 500 (SPY) and Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). Despite NMAI offering a massive distribution rate, I anticipate this underperformance to continue over the next decade. Therefore, I do not believe NMAI is a good fit for investors that want to capture the highest total return possible.

Valuation

Since NMAI operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the assets within. Therefore, NMAI currently trades at a discount to NAV of about 9%. Looking back over the fund's history below, we can see that NMAI frequently traded at a much larger discount to NAV since its inception. However, I don't really see any catalyst that could be causing the discount level to consistently shrink. The macro environment is still challenging and growth may be slower.

In order to get better clarity about the fund's performance, I reviewed the most recent annual report for 2024. Net investment income amounted to $0.37 per share, while net realized gains were $0.69 per share. Looking back on prior years, we can see that net investment income has remained fairly consistent to the 2023's performance. The history here is still quite short, so we can't effectively judge how resilient or strong the performance is here yet. However, I do want to mention that I want to eventually see net investment income levels improve to the point where it covers a more significant portion of the distributions.

As it stands, NMAI is pretty reliant on net realized gains to fuel much of the NAV growth and support the payouts. This leaves the fund vulnerable during market declines. If markets are declining, NMAI is also likely to experience losses. For instance, NMAI experienced net realized losses of $4.86 per share during the market decline of 2022. Since this large drop, the NAV hasn't been able to regain any positive growth momentum. For instance, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $20.03 per share
  • 2022: $13.41 per share
  • 2023: $13.76 per share
  • 2024: $13.17 per share

Something that I've noticed with closed end funds is that the discount or premium to NAV level is usually a direct reflection of the underlying performance over a longer period of time. If NMAI is able to preserve its NAV and keep payouts consistent, the fund may eventually trade in the premium to NAV territory. The current discount may reflect that the fund has deteriorated investor capital by nearly 28% since the merger in November 2021.

Risk Profile

Since NMAI implements debt securities as part of its portfolio, there is a risk related to borrower default. In order to produce the generous distribution rate that the fund does, NMAI invests in some debt securities with poor credit ratings. For reference, debt securities with a credit rating below BBB are considered to be below investment grade. These are usually borrowers that may have less consistent cash flows, may lean more heavily on debt, and may not be able to effectively navigate through headwinds. The breakdown of assets by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 53.9%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - CCC): 44.6%
  • Not Rated: 1.5%

This exposure to debt securities has led to NMAI being sensitive to changes in the interest rate environment. Looking at the chart below, we can see how NMAI's price rapidly declined throughout 2022 and 2023 as interest rates were hiked in an effort to combat record-breaking inflationary data. A higher cost of debt puts pressure on borrowers and shrinks operating margins, which can cause valuations to decline.

Additionally, NMAI implements leverage as part of its strategy, which can help to amplify returns when an attractive opportunity is present. The use of leverage currently amounts to 29.75% of assets, which I consider to be moderate. Ideally, I would like to see leverage remain below 20% to 25% during this higher interest rate environment. Since interest rates are high, the spread between the cost of leverage and yield received from investments may shrink, putting pressure on NMAI's operating margins as well. Investors must also consider the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated towards an investment that doesn't perform as anticipated.

If interest rates remain high, there's a chance that defaults could cause valuations to further decline. For instance, S&P Global estimates that slower economic growth and escalation of the tariff situation could cause defaults to increase to 3% by December 2025. However, this is only the estimated worst-case scenario. In a base scenario, defaults may only increase to 1.6% by the end of the year. The Fed has kept interest rates unchanged in their latest meeting as they continue to monitor the macroeconomic environment.

Questionable Dividend Sustainability

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.126 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 12.1%. If we multiply this monthly distribution by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $1.512 per share. Looking back on the annual earnings of NMAI so far, the fund has a mixed history of actually being able to generate earnings in excess of this distribution amount. For instance, here are the annual earnings of the fund so far:

  • 2021: $0.38 per share
  • 2022: ($4.39) per share
  • 2023: $1.65 per share
  • 2024: $1.06 per share

As we can see, NMAI has only been able to out earn the distribution in one of the last four years. While I would be more optimistic about the coverage if market conditions were ideal, the current track record makes me question whether or not NMAI will be able to support the payouts. I believe that the fund would actually benefit from a slight reduction in the payout.

A lower dividend payout would help preserve the health of the NAV and allow the fund to maintain some excess capital to better navigate headwinds. Looking at the chart below, we can see that the most recent payout looks a lot smaller. However, this is only because the fund paid out quarterly distributions in the past and only recently switched to monthly payouts.

The recent underperformance means that the fund has leaned heavily on the use of return of capital distributions over the last year. Return of capital distributions can be an indication that the underlying strategy isn't generating enough income to support the distributions. Therefore, NMAI is currently paying out distributions from its NAV, rather than from earnings it generated. This adds to the downward NAV pressure, further reinforcing the idea that a distribution cut would be beneficial.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that NMAI is an interesting-blended fund between equities and debt securities that can provide some attractive returns. However, the current market conditions don't necessarily align the fund for growth at this moment. The exposure to debt investments means that NMAI has demonstrated a sensitivity to the higher interest rate environment. This has caused earnings to shrink, and now the distribution coverage is questionable. I believe the fund would benefit from a distribution cut to help preserve the NAV and once conditions improve, I would consider a position in NMAI.
